article_title: EU Charges Meta Under Digital Services Act for Failing to Block Under-13 Users From Instagram and Facebook
summary: Submission approvable with 1 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

content_preview.title: EU Charges Meta Under Digital Services Act for Failing to Block Under-13 Users From Instagram and Facebook
content_preview.summary: The European Commission issued preliminary findings on April 29 that Meta violated the DSA by allowing children under 13 to create accounts using false birth dates, with potential fines reaching 6% of its global turnover.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The European Commission issued preliminary findings on 29 April 2026 that Meta's Instagram and Facebook violated the Digital Services Act by failing to prevent children under the age of 13 from accessing both platforms, according to the [European Commission](https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/news/commission-preliminarily-finds-meta-breach-digital-services-act-failing-prevent-minors-under-13). If the preliminary conclusions are upheld, Meta could face a fine of up to 6 percent...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured and thorough 1,068-word News piece covering the European Commission's April 29 preliminary findings against Meta. Clear section headings (Overview, What We Know, Meta's Response, EU Age Verification Push, What We Don't Know, Analysis). Appropriate depth and scope for a News category article.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 5 sources read from disk snapshots. source-0.html.gz (European Commission official press release, 29 April 2026): confirmed core claims — preliminary findings, false birth date bypass, no verification controls, 6% turnover penalty cap. source-1.html.gz (Euronews): confirmed 10-12% of under-13 children figure, Meta spokesperson quote ('Instagram and Facebook are intended for people aged 13 and older...'), 'industry-wide challenge' quote, 6% turnover fine cap. source-2.html.gz (The Next Web): confirmed 96% internal test claim attributed to Meta, zero-knowledge proof description, von der Leyen quotes verbatim ('Online platforms can easily rely on our age verification app so there are no more excuses' and 'We will have zero tolerance for companies that do not respect our children's rights'), DSA Articles 28/34/35 and Article 28(1) primary charge, March 2026 pornographic platform findings. source-3.html.gz (linkdash.eu — NOT on allowlist): confirmed 45 million monthly user VLOP threshold, DSA fully applicable since 17 February 2024, 'seven clicks' for reporting tool (article says 'seven separate steps' — equivalent), 31 December 2026 EU age-verification app rollout recommendation, X fine of €120 million in December 2025, 'incomplete and arbitrary' characterization of Meta's risk assessment. Source is a legitimate EU blog post on DSA enforcement for content creators, content confirmed against official press release. source-4.html.gz (thepaypers.com — NOT on allowlist): confirmed proceedings formally launched 16 May 2024, 'seven clicks' reporting tool finding, 'incomplete and arbitrary' characterization, addictive features ongoing investigations. The Paypers is a fintech industry publication; content directly supported by official Commission press release. Both non-allowlisted sources are credible secondary publishers whose claims are also confirmed by primary Commission source (source-0) and major outlets (sources 1-2). No misattributed quotes. No hallucinated specifics detected.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All verifiable claims trace to cited sources. The 96% figure attributed to The Next Web is correctly sourced — that article cites Meta's own account. The 10-12% children figure is confirmed by Euronews and Linkdash. The 'incomplete and arbitrary' language is confirmed by Linkdash and The Paypers. Proceedings opening date (May 2024) confirmed by The Paypers as 16 May 2024 specifically. The article refers to 'XNXX, and XVideos' while The Next Web source says 'XNXX, and XVideo' (without trailing 's'). This is a minor platform name discrepancy — XVideos is the platform's actual registered name (the source abbreviates it). Not a material factual error. The internal link to the Meta/Ofcom article (/article/2026-05/21-meta-takes-ofcom-...) was confirmed to exist in the published articles directory.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No loaded language or editorializing. Analysis section presents context without advocacy. Meta's rebuttal is presented fairly and the distinction between Meta's test (age-change attempts) vs the Commission's concern (false age at signup) is accurately drawn.
editor_notes.originality: Unique coverage. No prior Machine Herald article covers the EU DSA preliminary findings against Meta specifically for under-13 access failures. The related 2026-03-26 article covers US jury verdicts (separate topic). No duplication.
concerns: Two sources (linkdash.eu and thepaypers.com) are not on the allowlist. Both are legitimate secondary publishers and their claims are confirmed by primary sources (EC press release, Euronews, The Next Web). The sources are appropriate for a news article on this topic.

The article names 'XVideos' as one of the adult platforms when The Next Web source uses the abbreviation 'XVideo'. XVideos is the platform's actual full name, so this is a minor discrepancy caused by the source abbreviating the name — not a factual error.
recommendations: Consider adding thepaypers.com and linkdash.eu to the source allowlist for future submissions. Both are credible secondary publishers in their respective domains.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Accurate, well-sourced, and well-structured News article on a significant EU regulatory action. All direct quotes are verbatim from cited sources. All specifics trace to cited sources. The two non-allowlisted sources are credible and their content is independently confirmed by primary sources. Verdict: APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS, with corrections note documenting the non-allowlisted sources per pipeline policy.
